perturbation.changed_text: (n) "Territory" shall mean anywhere the Licensee deems commercially viable.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining 'Territory' to be 'anywhere the Licensee deems commercially viable' creates a contradiction. The original definition clearly limits the territory to mainland China. The new definition allows the Licensee to operate outside this area at their discretion, going against the spirit of the original agreement, opening potential for disputes on rights, royalties, and other legally binding aspects that were agreed upon within the context of operating exclusively in China. This creates uncertainty because the agreement hinges on a clear, geographically limited 'Territory'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles regarding Definiteness of Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts contract law principles requiring definiteness of key terms. By making the territory undefined and dependent on Licensee's discretion, it introduces ambiguity that can render the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modification creates a legal contradiction by rendering the definition of 'Territory' indefinite, violating contract law principles that require definiteness of key terms. The original contract specifies 'mainland China,' providing a clear geographical boundary. The change to 'anywhere the Licensee deems commercially viable' introduces ambiguity, potentially making the contract unenforceable. This aligns with the Law Explanation, which states that indefiniteness can render a contract unenforceable, and relates to the principle in Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-204, that a contract must show sufficient agreement, which is undermined by vague terms.

perturbation.original_text: In exchange for the Share Consideration, Licensor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual and non-perpetual license (subject to the duration and scope, and format limitations for which Licensor has the rights to each Title as specified in Schedule A1-A6 of Schedule A) to: i. license, exhibit, distribute, reproduce, transmit, perform, display, and otherwise exploit and make available each Title within the Territory in any language by VOD (including SVOD, TVOD, AVOD and free VOD) for Internet, TV and mobile platforms (including, but not limited to, OTT streaming services, Sites and Mobile Sites), subject to these limitations for each of the Title in Schedule A1-A6:
perturbation.changed_text: In exchange for the Share Consideration, Licensor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, royalty-free, license (subject to the duration and scope, and format limitations for which Licensor has the rights to each Title as specified in Schedule A1-A6 of Schedule A) to: i. license, exhibit, distribute, reproduce, transmit, perform, display, and otherwise exploit and make available each Title within the Territory in any language by VOD (including SVOD, TVOD, AVOD and free VOD) for Internet, TV and mobile platforms (including, but not limited to, OTT streaming services, Sites and Mobile Sites), subject to these limitations for each of the Title in Schedule A1-A6:
perturbation.explanation: Removing "perpetual and non-perpetual" from the license grant creates a contradiction. Without specifying the license duration, the contract becomes ambiguous, which can cause issues relating to copyright law and duration of ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Copyright Act of 1976 - Duration of Copyright
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts copyright law because copyright duration is statutorily defined. Removing the specification makes the license duration uncertain and conflicts with established copyright principles regarding the term of protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 302 - Duration of copyright: Works created on or after January 1, 1978 | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 302 - Duration of copyright: Works created on or after January 1, 1978 U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— Copyright in a work created on or after January 1, 1978 , subsists from its creation and, except as provided by the following subsections, endures for a term consisting of the life of the author and 70 years after the author’s death. (b) Joint Works.— In the case of a joint work prepared by two or more authors who did not work for hire, the copyright endures for a term consisting of the life of the last surviving author and 70 years after such last surviving author’s death. (c) Anonymous Works, Pseudonymous Works, and Works Made for Hire.— In the case of an anonymous work, a pseudonymous work, or a work made for hire, the copyright endures for a term of 95 years from the year of its first publication, or a term of 120 years from the year of its creation, whichever expires first. If, before the end of such term, the identity of one or more of the authors of an anonymous or pseudonymous work is revealed in the records of a registration made for that work under subsections (a) or (d) of section 408, or in the records provided by this subsection, the copyright in the work endures for the term specified by subsection (a) or (b), based on the life of the author or authors whose identity has been revealed. Any person having an interest in the copyright in an anonymous or pseudonymous work may at any time record, in records to be maintained by the Copyright Office for that purpose, a statement identifying one or more authors of the work; the statement shall also identify the person filing it, the nature of that person’s interest, the source of the information recorded, and the particular work affected, and shall comply in form and c

perturbation.justification: The removal of "perpetual and non-perpetual" introduces ambiguity regarding the license duration, conflicting with copyright law's requirement for a defined term as per 17 U.S. Code § 302. The modified contract provides no clarity on whether the license extends for the copyright term, as defined by the life of the author plus 70 years (or other statutory periods). The absence of duration specification directly contradicts copyright law which establishes defined periods for copyright protection.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon written notice if the other party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after the date that it receives written notice of such breach from the non-breaching party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time, with or without cause, with no notice needed.
perturbation.explanation: The clause regarding breach and cure period is replaced with 'at any time, with or without cause, with no notice needed'. This makes the contract at-will, and introduces contradiction with contract principles, where specific performance is required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification eliminates the requirement for 'material breach' and 'cure period' before termination, effectively allowing either party to terminate at will. While at-will contracts are permissible, they are still subject to the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Removing the breach clause suggests a lack of good faith, as a party could terminate for arbitrary reasons, potentially harming the other party's interests. This contradicts the legal expectation of fair dealing in contractual relationships.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text permits termination 'at any time, with or without cause, with no notice needed'. This directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith, which is part of contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By allowing termination without cause or notice, the modified text removes the need for good faith, creating a clear contradiction.
